text: Welcome to this video on a right lower sleeve lobectomy with Mr. Joel Dunning and Marco Nardini at James Cook University Hospital. The patient is a sixty five year old male smoker with a history of HIV since nineteen ninety nine which is well controlled. A CT guided biopsy showed an adenocarcinoma of the right lower lobe.

The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary ligament to access the R9 pulmonary ligament nodes, sampling a small node. The dissection proceeds to the station 7 lymph nodes, where a large malignant-looking node is lifted by a cat ear and dissected with Maryland forceps. The node is completely removed, with visible portions being extracted.

The surgeon removes additional parts of the lymph node and continues dissection. The anterior part of the oblique fissure is dissected using Maryland bipolar forceps diathermy. The Maryland forceps pass under the fissure, pulling a sling through, which allows the assistant to pass the leader catheter. The leader catheter guides the Covidian curved tip purple sixty stapler to divide the anterior part of the oblique fissure. The right inferior pulmonary artery is dissected using Maryland forceps and cadire.

The surgeon uses the cadire to maneuver underneath the pulmonary artery, pulling a yellow sling through to guide the stapler. The leader catheter is passed under the artery, facilitating the stapling process. The curved tip of the stapler allows for easy maneuvering over the vessel. The Cavidian Curve Tip Tan-Forty-Five staple is used to divide the pulmonary artery, providing a clear view of the divided right inferior pulmonary artery. The apical branch of the right inferior pulmonary artery is visible behind it. The Maryland forceps are employed for precise dissection around the vessel, creating a passage for the leader catheter, which guides the stapler over the vessel again. The Cavidian Curve Tip Tan-Forty-Five staple is used to divide this artery, showing both divided arteries.

The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to dissect the right inferior pulmonary vein, while long graspers and cadier are employed to maneuver the lobe for improved access. The cadier passes through a space behind the vein, pulling a yellow sling to guide the leader catheter and stapler into position. A curved tip TAN-Forty-Five staple is used to divide the vein, and the divided inferior pulmonary vein is visible. The procedure progresses to the posterior aspect of the oblique fissure, where long graspers pull the leader catheter through.

The surgeon uses CADIR and lung graspers to manipulate the lobe into the staple jaws, showcasing the robotic arms' utility in maneuvering lobe tissue. The Maryland forceps guide the leader catheter to the right lower lobe bronchus, which is cut with scissors due to the biopsy location. The divided inferior pulmonary artery is visible in the foreground. The specimen bag is introduced through the utility port, and the right lower lobe is placed into the bag for removal.

The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to oversew the remaining orifice of the right lower lobe bronchus. The needle is threaded through an eyelet at the end of the suture and pulled down to tighten it into place, demonstrating the barbed nature of the suture that prevents loosening.

The surgeon revisits the suture line to ensure security and minimize air leaks. After confirming malignancy in the frozen section of the bronchus, stitches are removed, and a sleeve resection is performed. The right middle lobe bronchus is cut just beyond its origin from the bronchus intermedius, with the cadier holding back the bronchus for optimal access. The right middle bronchus is cut completely around, followed by cutting the distal end of the right bronchus intermedius proximal to its bifurcation. The extended margin of the right lower lobe bronchus is visible, with the orifices of the right middle bronchus and right bronchus intermedius in the background.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the right middle bronchus and the right bronchus intermedius using V-lock sutures, which are visible as they secure the connection without requiring knots.

A single silk stitch is placed to retract the middle bronchus, followed by continuous suturing for the anastomosis. The robotic system allows the surgeon to suture ambidextrously, using the left hand with filtered tremor control.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to perform suturing, taking even bites to bring the two ends of the bronchus together. The anastomosis is completed with the final knots tied. The cavity is filled with warm saline, and the lung is inflated to check for air leaks. Bubbles are observed, prompting the addition of more sutures to secure the bronchial reconstruction. After retesting, no air leaks are detected.

This is a diagram overviewing the procedure. First, the right lower lobe bronchus was cut, giving the margin in red, but after a positive frozen section, the margins were then extended all the way to the distal part of the bronchus intermedius, giving the margins in green. To achieve these margins, the right bronchus intermedius was cut just before its bifurcation, and the right middle lobe bronchus was cut proximally, allowing the right lower lobe bronchus to be entirely removed. The right middle or bronchus was then attached onto the right bronchus intermedius through a sleeve reconstruction. The patient went home on post-op day two.
Histology showed a primary adenocarcinoma with metastasis to station seven nodes. Thank you for watching our video.
